pr:section.pr:title: Testing Layer
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: First-class test definitions as spec nodes, with distinct test types, content references, test suites, and an extensible witness model.
pr:section.pr:p: Specifications declare what a system should do. Plans decompose that intent into steps. But neither captures how to verify that the implementation is correct. The testing layer fills this gap: it elevates tests from external scripts and ad hoc shell commands into first-class spec nodes with IDs, relations, and artifact mappings. Tests become part of the knowledge base, queryable and traceable like any other spec element.
The layer defines distinct test types, each modeling a different verification paradigm:

pr:property.pr:propdesc: Human-executed procedures with expected results and optional role assignment.
Structured input/output data pairs, with each value referencing a cnt:content node for the actual data.
Generative/property-based tests declaring invariants that must hold over generated inputs, with optional bounds on sample count and timeout.
Executable test code referenced through cnt:content, with a language attribute for runtime selection.
Blackbox external verification via shell commands, executable scripts, or API calls.

pr:section.pr:p: Tests reference data through cnt:content nodes rather than embedding it inline. This separation keeps test definitions declarative (what to verify) while the content layer holds the actual data (the inputs, outputs, and source code). A single content node can be referenced by multiple tests, avoiding duplication.
The tst:suite element groups tests into logical collections. Each suite references test nodes by ID, enabling selective execution and reporting without duplicating test definitions.

The testing layer absorbs the plan layer's witness concept. Where plans previously embedded pln:witness elements inside acceptance criteria, they now reference tests via a test attribute on pln:criterion. This means a single test definition can serve as the witness for multiple plan criteria, and tests exist independently of any particular plan. The witness type enumeration (tst:WitnessKind) is extensible: extension layers define new witness types annotated with tst:witness-type-properties, activated via a rel:relation type="refines" edge to type-witness-kind.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding how the testing layer provides first-class test definitions with five distinct types, content references, suites, and an extensible witness model that replaces embedded plan witnesses.

pr:section.pr:title: Manual Tests
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Human-executed test procedures with structured steps and expected results.
pr:section.pr:p: Manual tests capture verification procedures that require human judgment: visual inspections, UX assessments, security reviews, or any check that cannot be reduced to a deterministic program. Each manual test has a procedure (the steps to follow) and an expected result (what should be observed). Both fields support mixed content with xs:any for embedding prose, terminology references, and source citations.
The optional role element specifies who should perform the test (e.g., "security engineer", "QA lead"), enabling assignment and filtering in test management workflows.

pr:example.pr:title: Manual accessibility review
pr:example.pr:codeblock: <tst:manual id="test-accessibility-review"> <tst:title>Screen reader navigation</tst:title> <tst:procedure> <pr:steps> <pr:step>Enable VoiceOver on macOS</pr:step> <pr:step>Navigate to the document list view</pr:step> <pr:step>Tab through all interactive elements</pr:step> </pr:steps> </tst:procedure> <tst:expected> <pr:p>All interactive elements are reachable and announce their purpose. No focus traps.</pr:p> </tst:expected> <tst:role>accessibility-engineer</tst:role> </tst:manual>

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding manual test structure: procedure steps, expected results, role assignment, and mixed content support.

pr:section.pr:title: Test Vectors
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Structured input/output test data pairs referencing content nodes.
pr:section.pr:p: Vector tests define concrete input/output pairs for deterministic verification. Each tst:vectors element contains one or more tst:vector children, each with labeled input and output data references. The actual data lives in cnt:content nodes, referenced by the content attribute. This indirection keeps test structure separate from test data, and allows the same data to be reused across multiple tests.

Vectors support multiple inputs and outputs per test case, enabling multi-parameter scenarios. Each input and output carries an optional label attribute that names what the data represents (e.g., "query", "stdin", "response-body"). Labels disambiguate when a vector has more than one input or output.

Each vector has an expected outcome: pass (output matches, the default), fail (system rejects the input), error (system signals an error condition), or skip (vector is disabled). This enables negative testing: vectors where the correct behavior is rejection or failure, not just successful output matching.

Individual vectors carry their own IDs, making each test case independently addressable for cross-referencing, artifact mapping, and selective execution. Space-separated tags on each vector enable categorization (edge-case, regression, boundary, smoke) for filtering and reporting. A rich description element with mixed content provides context for what the case exercises.

pr:example.pr:title: Vector test with reusable content
pr:example.pr:codeblock:
<!-- Content nodes for test data --> <cnt:content id="cnt-valid-xml" media-type="application/xml" hash="sha256:abc..."> <cnt:body><root><child/></root></cnt:body> </cnt:content> <cnt:content id="cnt-valid-parse-tree" media-type="application/json" hash="sha256:def..."> <cnt:body>{"tag":"root","children":[{"tag":"child"}]}</cnt:body> </cnt:content> <cnt:content id="cnt-malformed-xml" media-type="application/xml" hash="sha256:ghi..."> <cnt:body><root><unclosed</cnt:body> </cnt:content> <cnt:content id="cnt-parse-error-msg" media-type="text/plain" hash="sha256:jkl..."> <cnt:body>expected closing tag at line 1</cnt:body> </cnt:content>
<!-- Vector test with multiple cases --> <tst:vectors id="test-xml-parser"> <tst:title>XML parser conformance</tst:title> <tst:description> Verifies correct parsing of well-formed XML and proper error reporting for malformed input. </tst:description>
<tst:vector id="vec-simple-doc" tags="happy-path"> <tst:input content="cnt-valid-xml" label="document"/> <tst:output content="cnt-valid-parse-tree" label="ast"/> </tst:vector>
<tst:vector id="vec-malformed" tags="edge-case error"> <tst:input content="cnt-malformed-xml" label="document"/> <tst:output content="cnt-parse-error-msg" label="error-message"/> <tst:outcome>error</tst:outcome> <tst:description>Unclosed tag should produce a clear error message with line number.</tst:description> </tst:vector> </tst:vectors>

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding vector test structure: input/output pairs, content node references, per-vector IDs, and data reuse.

pr:section.pr:title: Property Tests
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Generative/property-based tests declaring invariants over generated inputs.
pr:section.pr:p: Property tests specify invariants that must hold for any valid input, rather than enumerating specific cases. Each tst:property element declares an invariant (the condition that must hold), an optional generator (how to produce test inputs), and optional bounds constraining the test execution (sample count, timeout duration).

Property tests complement vector tests: vectors verify known cases exhaustively, while properties verify general laws across a potentially infinite input space. Both the invariant and generator fields support mixed content for rich descriptions with embedded prose and terminology references.

pr:example.pr:title: Property test bounds
pr:example.pr:codeblock: <tst:property id="test-c14n-roundtrip"> <tst:title>Canonicalization is idempotent</tst:title> <tst:invariant> For any well-formed XML document d, c14n(c14n(d)) == c14n(d). </tst:invariant> <tst:generator> Well-formed XML documents with varying namespace declarations, attribute ordering, whitespace, and comment placement. Include documents with: 0 elements, deeply nested elements (100+ depth), mixed content, CDATA sections, and processing instructions. </tst:generator> <tst:bounds sample-count="5000" timeout="PT30S"/> </tst:property>

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding property test structure: invariants, generators, execution bounds, and how they complement vector tests.

pr:section.pr:title: Program Tests
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Executable test code referenced through content nodes.
pr:section.pr:p: Program tests are executable: the source attribute references a cnt:content node containing the actual test code. The content node may hold the code inline or reference an external file. The optional lang attribute specifies the programming language, enabling tooling to select the appropriate runtime or syntax highlighting.

Program tests are the most flexible test type. They can implement anything a test framework supports: unit tests, integration tests, snapshot tests, benchmark assertions. By referencing content nodes rather than embedding code inline, the test definition remains a clean declarative node while the implementation lives in the content layer.

pr:example.pr:title: Program test backed by content
pr:example.pr:codeblock:
<!-- Test code embedded in a content node --> <cnt:content id="cnt-validate-test" media-type="text/x-rust" hash="sha256:abc..."> <cnt:body><![CDATA[ #[test] fn validate_rejects_duplicate_ids() { let spec = r#"<spec:clayers ...> <pr:section id="dup"/> <pr:section id="dup"/> </spec:clayers>"#; let result = validate_spec_str(spec); assert!(result.has_errors()); assert!(result.errors[0].message.contains("duplicate")); } ]]></cnt:body> </cnt:content>
<tst:program id="test-validate-dup-ids" source="cnt-validate-test" lang="rust"> <tst:title>Validator rejects duplicate IDs</tst:title> </tst:program>
<!-- Test code in an external file --> <cnt:content id="cnt-drift-tests" media-type="text/x-rust" url="file:crates/clayers-spec/src/drift.rs" hash="sha256:def..."/>
<tst:program id="test-drift-detection" source="cnt-drift-tests" lang="rust"> <tst:title>Drift detection integration tests</tst:title> </tst:program>

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding program test structure: content references for source code, language attributes, and the separation between test declaration and test implementation.

pr:section.pr:title: Witness Tests
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Blackbox external verification via commands, scripts, or API calls.
pr:section.pr:p: Witness tests verify outcomes through external interaction rather than embedded logic. The type attribute classifies the witness mechanism:

pr:property.pr:propdesc: A shell command whose exit code indicates pass (0) or fail (non-zero). The command child element holds the command string, and the optional exit-code element overrides the default success code.
An executable script referenced via the source attribute, pointing to a cnt:content node. The lang attribute specifies the script language for runtime selection.
An HTTP API call with endpoint, method, and expected status code. Request and response bodies reference cnt:content nodes via request and response attributes.

pr:section.pr:p: The witness type enumeration is extensible. Extension layers define new witness types by creating a complexType annotated with tst:witness-type-properties and activating it via a rel:relation type="refines" edge to type-witness-kind. This follows the same extensibility pattern used for relation types and source types.

pr:example.pr:title: Witness tests and plan criteria
pr:example.pr:codeblock:
<!-- Command witness: simple shell check --> <tst:witness id="test-cargo-test" type="command"> <tst:title>Cargo test suite passes</tst:title> <tst:command>cargo test --workspace</tst:command> </tst:witness>
<!-- Script witness: complex verification via content node --> <cnt:content id="cnt-perf-script" media-type="text/x-python" hash="sha256:abc..."> <cnt:body><![CDATA[ import subprocess, json, sys result = subprocess.run(["cargo", "bench", "--", "--output-format=json"], capture_output=True, text=True) data = json.loads(result.stdout) if any(b["ns/iter"] > 1000 for b in data["benchmarks"]): sys.exit(1) ]]></cnt:body> </cnt:content>
<tst:witness id="test-perf-regression" type="script" source="cnt-perf-script" lang="python"> <tst:title>No performance regressions</tst:title> </tst:witness>
<!-- API witness: health check with expected response --> <cnt:content id="cnt-health-response" media-type="application/json" hash="sha256:def..."> <cnt:body>{"status":"healthy"}</cnt:body> </cnt:content>
<tst:witness id="test-api-health" type="api" response="cnt-health-response"> <tst:title>API health endpoint</tst:title> <tst:endpoint>https://api.example.com/health</tst:endpoint> <tst:method>GET</tst:method> <tst:status>200</tst:status> </tst:witness>
<!-- Referencing tests from plan criteria --> <pln:criterion test="test-cargo-test"> All tests pass before release. </pln:criterion>

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding witness test structure: the three built-in witness kinds (command, script, api), content references for scripts and API payloads, and the extensibility mechanism for new witness types.

trm:term.trm:name: WitnessKind
trm:term.trm:definition: The XSD simpleType that enumerates witness type values for test verification. Defines command, script, and api as built-in witness kinds. Extensible via tst:witness-type-properties annotations and refines relations.

trm:term.trm:name: WitnessType
trm:term.trm:definition: The XSD complexType that defines the structure of witness test elements. Each witness has a type (classified by WitnessKind), a title, and type-specific children: command/exit-code for command witnesses, source/lang for script witnesses, and endpoint/method/status/request/response for API witnesses.

rel:relation.rel:note: The testing layer adds first-class test definitions as a semantic dimension within the layered architecture.
Manual tests are one of the test types in the testing layer.
Test vectors are one of the test types in the testing layer.
Property tests are one of the test types in the testing layer.
Program tests are one of the test types in the testing layer.
Witness tests are one of the test types in the testing layer.
The WitnessKind simpleType is the schema definition for witness type classification in the testing layer.
The WitnessType complexType is the schema definition for witness test elements in the testing layer.
The testing layer depends on the content layer for test data references: vector inputs/outputs, program source code, and witness script and API payloads are all stored as cnt:content nodes.

spec:clayers.llm:node: Describes the testing layer: first-class test definitions as spec nodes with five distinct types (manual, vectors, property, program, witness). Tests reference data through cnt:content nodes. Suites group tests by ID reference. The testing layer absorbs the plan layer's witness concept: plans now reference tests via pln:criterion/@test instead of embedding pln:witness elements. The WitnessKind enum is extensible via tst:witness-type-properties annotations and refines relations to type-witness-kind.

Describes manual tests: human-executed procedures with structured steps, expected results, and optional role assignment. Mixed content support via xs:any for embedding prose and terminology references. Used for verification that requires human judgment (visual inspections, UX assessments, security reviews).

Describes test vectors: structured input/output data pairs with labeled data references, expected outcomes (pass/fail/error/skip), tags for categorization, and rich descriptions. Multiple inputs and outputs per vector support multi-parameter test cases. Data lives in cnt:content nodes. Individual vectors carry IDs for addressability.

Describes property tests: generative/invariant-based testing. Declares invariants that must hold over generated inputs. Optional generator describes input production. Optional bounds constrain execution (sample-count, timeout). Complements vector tests: vectors verify known cases, properties verify general laws.

Describes program tests: executable test code. The source attribute references a cnt:content node containing test code (inline or external). The lang attribute specifies the programming language for runtime selection. Separates test declaration from test implementation.

Describes witness tests: blackbox external verification. Three built-in kinds: command (shell exit code), script (executable via cnt:content reference with lang), api (HTTP call with endpoint, method, status, request/response content references). The witness type enumeration is extensible via tst:witness-type-properties and refines relations.
spec:clayers.llm:schema: The testing schema defines distinct test types as first-class spec nodes. tst:manual for human-executed procedures, tst:vectors for input/output data pairs via cnt:content references, tst:property for generative invariant-based tests, tst:program for executable test code via cnt:content references, and tst:witness for blackbox external verification (command, script, api). Tests are grouped by tst:suite. The WitnessKind enum is extensible via tst:witness-type-properties annotations. Plans reference tests via pln:criterion/@test instead of embedding witnesses.

A human-executed test with procedure steps and expected results. Embeds prose layer elements via xs:any for rich content. Optional role element specifies who performs the test. The id makes it a node addressable by suites and plan criteria.

Structured test data vectors. Contains vector children, each with labeled input and output data references (content attr -> cnt:content, optional label attr). Supports multiple inputs/outputs per vector for multi-parameter cases. Expected outcome (pass/fail/error/skip), tags for categorization, and rich descriptions. Individual vectors have their own IDs for addressability.

Generative/property-based test. Declares an invariant that must hold over generated inputs. Optional generator describes input production. Optional bounds element constrains execution via sample-count and timeout attributes. Mixed content + xs:any throughout.

Executable test code. The source attribute references a cnt:content node containing the test code. The lang attribute specifies the programming language. Test declaration is separate from implementation: the node describes what to test, the content node holds the code.

Blackbox external verification. The type attribute classifies the witness via WitnessKind: command (shell command + exit code), script (cnt:content reference + lang), api (endpoint + method + status + request/response content references). Extensible via tst:witness-type-properties.
Groups tests into logical collections. Each test child references a test node by ID via the ref attribute. Suites enable selective execution and reporting without duplicating test definitions.
spec:clayers.art:exempt: Conceptual documentation describing the testing layer's purpose, test types, and witness model. No code implements this concept.
Conceptual documentation describing manual test structure. No code implements this concept.
Conceptual documentation describing vector test structure. No code implements this concept.
Conceptual documentation describing property test structure. No code implements this concept.
Conceptual documentation describing program test structure. No code implements this concept.
Conceptual documentation describing witness test structure. No code implements this concept.

art:mapping.art:note: WitnessKind simpleType: command, script, api witness types.
WitnessType complexType: type-classified witness with command, script, and API children and attributes.
